blocks.text: Ms. Debra Vasquez, VP Finance
{{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}
Re: Site Grading Design, Esparza-Lamb Site
Dear Ms. Vasquez:

Thank you for the opportunity to submit this letter of agreement for site grading design services in connection with the Esparza-Lamb development. This letter sets out the scope of our services, the fee, the schedule, and the terms under which this firm proposes to perform the work. Upon your countersignature it will constitute the agreement between {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}} and Brackenridge Civil Group Inc.
Scope of Services

This firm will prepare a site grading design for the property in a form suitable for submittal to the reviewing agency and for construction. The work includes review of the topographic survey and the geotechnical information furnished by the Client; establishment of finished floor and pavement elevations; design of the grading plan, including cut and fill limits, slopes, and spot elevations at pavement, walks, and building perimeters; preparation of an earthwork quantity estimate for the graded condition; design of the stormwater conveyance necessary to serve the graded site, with supporting computations; preparation of an erosion and sediment control plan and details; and coordination of the grading design with the utility layout at those points where the two govern one another.

The work product will be a sealed plan set, together with the drainage computations and the earthwork estimate. This firm will prepare the submittal package and will respond to review comments from the reviewing agency through one review cycle. Field staking, construction observation, materials testing, boundary or topographic surveying, geotechnical investigation, permit fees, and the design of off-site improvements are not included in this scope, and this firm will not perform them except under a written amendment to this agreement.
Approach

We begin with the survey and the soils information and confirm that they agree with what is on the ground before any grading is drawn. Where they do not, we will contact you and identify what additional information is required. The grading design then proceeds from the drainage. We establish the outfall and the conveyance first and fit the finished contours to them, rather than grade the site and afterward look for a place to send the water. Preliminary grading will be brought to you for review before the plan set is developed in detail, so that any change in the finished floor elevations or in the pavement layout is made at the point where it is still inexpensive to make. Interim prints are issued for review and are not for construction. Only the sealed set governs.
Project Team

I will serve as Principal Engineer on this project and will seal the drawings. Linda Nguyen, Design Technician, will prepare the grading and utility sheets and will assemble the plan set. Jason Jordan, Design Technician, will prepare the drainage computations and the erosion and sediment control design, and is responsible for the standards under which the set is produced. I am the point of contact for any question of scope, schedule, or fee, and correspondence on those subjects should be directed to me. On matters of drawing content you may contact Linda Nguyen or Jason Jordan directly, and I would ask only that this office be copied, so that the project file remains complete.
Fee

For the services described above this firm will perform the work for a lump sum fee of {{fact:f:E-2001-001.fee}}. The fee includes ordinary reproduction, delivery, and telephone charges. Services outside the scope described above, including review cycles beyond the one provided, revisions arising from a change in the site plan after the preliminary grading has been accepted by the Client, and attendance at public hearings, will be performed only upon your written authorization and will be billed in addition to the lump sum fee at this firm's standard hourly rates then in effect.
Schedule

This firm is prepared to commence the work on {{fact:f:E-2001-001.start}}, subject to receipt of the countersigned agreement and of the topographic survey and the geotechnical report. We estimate approximately eight months from commencement to completion of the submittal and of our response to review comments. That estimate assumes the timely furnishing of information by the Client and does not include time consumed in agency review, which is outside this firm's control.
Fees and Payment

This firm will invoice monthly on the basis of the percentage of the work completed as of the invoice date. Invoices are due and payable within thirty (30) days. Amounts remaining unpaid after thirty (30) days will bear interest at the rate of one percent (1%) per month. Should any invoice remain unpaid for sixty (60) days, this firm may, upon seven (7) days' written notice, suspend performance until the account is brought current, and this firm will not be responsible for any delay so occasioned.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect upon your countersignature and continues until the services described above are complete. Either party may terminate upon fourteen (14) days' written notice to the other. Upon termination the Client will pay this firm for services performed and expenses incurred through the effective date of termination. Documents prepared under this agreement and delivered prior to termination remain subject to its terms.
Confidentiality

This firm will hold in confidence the site information, financial information, and development plans furnished by the Client and identified as confidential, and will not disclose them to third parties except as required in the ordinary conduct of the work, including submittals to the reviewing agency, or as required by law. This obligation does not extend to information that is or becomes public through no act of this firm.
Limitation of Liability

This firm's services will be performed with the care and skill ordinarily exercised by civil engineers practicing under similar conditions in this locality at this time. No other warranty, express or implied, is made. To the fullest extent permitted by law, the total liability of this firm and of its employees to the Client for all claims arising out of this agreement, whether in contract, in tort, or otherwise, is limited to the amount of the lump sum fee stated above.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which the project site is located. The parties will attempt in good faith to resolve any dispute arising under this agreement by negotiation between their principals before either commences an action.

This letter, together with the scope described above, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties on this subject and supersedes any prior discussion or proposal. If it correctly states our understanding, please sign both copies where indicated below, retain one for your file, and return the other to this office. We appreciate the confidence {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}} has placed in this firm, and we will be glad to begin.
Very truly yours,
To: Jason Jordan, Design Technician; Mark Barrett, Office Administrator
From: Linda Nguyen, Design Technician
Re: Site Grading Design, Esparza-Lamb site: project kickoff

The agreement came back countersigned and the job is open on the books as of {{fact:f:E-2001-001.start}}. This memo sets out the objective, the workstreams, and who has what, so that we are all working from the same understanding of the job rather than three understandings of it. The objective is a sealed grading plan set for {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}, with the drainage computations and the earthwork estimate, in a condition the reviewing agency will accept on the first submittal. We have roughly eight months. That sounds like a great deal of time and it is not, because the front of it belongs to the survey and the soils report, and we control neither one.

Debra Vasquez is the client contact. Anything touching scope, schedule, or fee goes out over the Principal Engineer's signature and not from us directly. If she telephones one of us with a question about drawing content, we answer it and then write down that the call happened and what was said, and the note goes in the folder the same day. A conversation nobody recorded did not happen, six months from now, when somebody asks why an elevation is what it is.
Workstreams

items: Base information. Topographic survey and geotechnical report from Esparza-Lamb, checked against the field before anything is drawn. Linda Nguyen. Anything missing or contradictory gets written up, not worked around.
Drainage. Outfall and conveyance established first; computations to follow the method the reviewing agency accepts, not the one we like. Jason Jordan.

Grading design. Finished floor and pavement elevations, cut and fill limits, slopes, spot elevations at pavement, walks, and building perimeters. Linda Nguyen, with the contours fitted to the conveyance rather than the reverse.
Erosion and sediment control. Plan and details. Jason Jordan.
Utility coordination. Where the grading and the utility layout govern one another, resolved on paper before either is called final. Linda Nguyen and Jason Jordan together.
Plan set production. Sheet order, lettering, match lines, and the cover. The CAD standards and the plotting setup are Jason Jordan's; he has the layer discipline and the rest of us will follow it.
Project file. Lettered folder, correspondence, transmittals, and scanning as it comes in. Mark Barrett.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Request the topographic survey and the geotechnical report in writing this week. Mark Barrett will type the request for signature and will chase it if it does not arrive, which is a thing he is good at.
Set up the lettered folder and the drawing template. Jason Jordan, by the end of next week, before anyone opens a sheet.
Walk the site once the survey is in hand. Linda Nguyen and Jason Jordan, together, one afternoon, with a print and a pencil.
Preliminary grading to the client for review before any sheet is developed in detail. Nothing leaves this office that has not been checked by someone who did not draw it.
No sheet goes out under the seal until the set is right. That is not our call to make, and we should not put anyone in the position of making it under a deadline.

blocks.text: One last item, and I will keep saying it. Redlines are kept. When a change is made, the marked-up print goes in the folder and not in the wastebasket. The set we send is a record of the decisions somebody made, and the only way to defend a decision later is to be able to show the print on which it was made.
Site Grading Design for Esparza-Lamb

items: Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}
Progress briefing to the client
Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Engagement commenced {{fact:f:E-2001-001.start}}
Presented by Linda Nguyen, Design Technician

blocks.text: Scope and Objective

items: A sealed grading plan set, suitable for agency submittal and for construction
Finished floor and pavement elevations, cut and fill limits, slopes, spot elevations
Stormwater conveyance sized to serve the graded site, with supporting computations
Erosion and sediment control plan and details

blocks.text: Status of the Work

items: Topographic survey and geotechnical report received and checked against the field before drawing began
Two disagreements between the survey and the existing conditions were written up and resolved rather than worked around
Outfall confirmed with the reviewing agency by telephone, and the conveyance layout established
Drainage computations under way, prepared by Jason Jordan against the method the agency accepts
Preliminary grading in progress; it comes to you for review before any sheet is developed in detail

blocks.text: Approach to the Grading

items: Drainage first: the outfall and the conveyance are set before the finished contours are drawn
Contours are fitted to the conveyance, not the reverse, which is what keeps the earthwork honest
Preliminary grading comes to you for review while a change is still inexpensive to make
Interim prints are issued for review and are not for construction
Only the sealed cover sheet governs; nothing is built from a check print

blocks.text: Matters Requiring Client Direction

items: Finished floor elevation at the north building: accept the imported fill, or shift the pad
Cross slope at the loading area, which governs the cut along the east side; the two cannot be traded independently
Confirmation that the utility layout is final; the grading and the utilities govern one another at three locations
Direction from Debra Vasquez on the above would let the preliminary grading be issued for review

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Preliminary grading to Esparza-Lamb for review once the pad elevations are confirmed
Drainage computations completed and checked against the method the agency accepts
Erosion and sediment control plan developed alongside the grading, not after it
Utility coordination meeting to close the three points where the layouts conflict
Plan set assembly to begin after the preliminary grading has been accepted

blocks.text: Project: Site Grading Design for {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}. Working session held at this office. Minutes taken by Jason Jordan and circulated for correction; corrections received within one week will be incorporated, after which these minutes stand as the record of the meeting.
Attendees

items: Linda Nguyen, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Jason Jordan, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Debra Vasquez, VP Finance, Esparza-Lamb

blocks.text: Discussion

The preliminary grading was reviewed print in hand. Linda Nguyen walked the plan from the outfall back through the site and explained the order in which the surface was set: conveyance first, contours fitted to it. Debra Vasquez asked whether the finished floor at the north building could be lowered to reduce the imported fill. The answer given was that it could, that the cost of doing so is paid in the cross slope at the loading area and in the cut along the east side, and that the two cannot be traded independently. She asked for the earthwork consequence of each of the two cases in writing before she decides. That is a reasonable request and it was accepted.

Drainage computations were reviewed in draft. The method used is the one the reviewing agency accepts, which was confirmed by telephone with the agency before the computations were begun rather than after. The outfall condition was discussed at some length. There is no disagreement about the outfall itself. There is a question about the tailwater assumption, and the assumption presently carried is the conservative one. Lowering it would relieve one structure. It was agreed not to lower it, on the ground that the relief is small and the argument at review would not be.

The utility layout was discussed. Three locations remain where the grading and the utilities govern one another and neither is final. Debra Vasquez stated that the utility layout is expected to be confirmed shortly but is not confirmed today. It was noted, without objection, that the plan set cannot be assembled against a layout that may still move, and that assembling it early would cost more in reissued sheets than waiting will cost in schedule.
Erosion and sediment control was reviewed briefly. The plan follows the grading and is developed alongside it. Nothing in it is presently controversial.

Schedule was discussed. The work is where it was expected to be. No party asserted a delay. Debra Vasquez asked to be told promptly if the pad decision, once made, moves the schedule; she was told that it would.
Action Items

rows: Earthwork quantities for both pad cases, in writing, for the client's decision
Prior to the next working session
Confirm the utility layout, or identify what remains open at the three governing locations
Complete the drainage computations at the tailwater assumption as carried; no change
Prior to the next working session
Hold the plan set assembly until the utility layout is confirmed
Distribute these minutes and file the marked-up preliminary print in the project folder

blocks.text: The marked-up print from this session is retained in the project folder. It is the record of what was decided and why, and it is not to be discarded once the changes are made.
Progress report for the site grading design prepared for {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}. This report covers the period since the last report and is submitted with the invoice for the same period.
Status Summary

The work is approximately at its midpoint and is proceeding as planned. The base information is in hand and has been checked. The drainage design is settled and the computations are complete and checked. The preliminary grading has been reviewed with the client, the pad question raised at that review has been answered in writing with the earthwork consequence of each case, and the design is now proceeding on the case the client selected. Assembly of the plan set has begun. Against the lump sum fee of {{fact:f:E-2001-001.fee}}, this firm has earned approximately one half as of the date of this report, which is consistent with the progress described. No condition presently known requires a change in the fee or in the scope.
Accomplishments This Period

Earthwork quantities were developed for both pad cases and issued to Esparza-Lamb in writing, as requested at the working session. The selected case was returned and the grading design has been carried forward on it. The finished floor and pavement elevations are set. The cut and fill limits are drawn. The spot elevations at pavement, walks, and building perimeters are placed and have been checked once by a second pair of eyes.

The drainage computations are complete. Jason Jordan carried them at the tailwater assumption agreed at the working session, which is the conservative one, and they have been checked against the method the reviewing agency accepts. The erosion and sediment control plan is developed and its details are drawn. The utility layout was confirmed by the client during this period, which released the three locations where the grading and the utilities governed one another; all three are now resolved on paper and the resolutions are reflected on the sheets.

With the layout confirmed, the plan set is being assembled. Sheet order is established, the lettering and the match lines are set, and the sheets are being brought up in the order in which they will be bound rather than in the order in which they happen to be finished. The cover sheet is drawn but is not sealed and will not be until the set beneath it is complete and checked. The marked-up prints from the client review and from the working session are retained in the project folder against the day someone asks why an elevation is what it is.
Upcoming Work

The remaining design work is the completion of the sheets now in progress and the earthwork estimate for the graded condition as finally designed. Following that, the set goes into check as a set, which is a different exercise from checking a sheet: the sheets have to agree with one another, and it is at the match lines that they usually do not. Linda Nguyen will run the check. The submittal package will then be assembled, comprising the set, the drainage computations, the earthwork estimate, and the transmittal, and the set will go to the Principal Engineer for seal. He reads what he signs, and the time for that is in the schedule and is not to be compressed. Once submitted, this firm will respond to the reviewing agency's comments through the one review cycle the agreement provides.
Risks

Agency review time is outside this firm's control and is not included in the eight months estimated for our work. This is not a new risk and it has not changed; it is stated here because it remains the largest single unknown in the schedule.

A change in the site plan after this point would be a change in scope. The pad decision is made and the utility layout is confirmed, and the grading is being drawn against both. If either moves, sheets already checked would be reissued, and the work would be performed under the agreement's provisions for authorized additional services rather than within the lump sum. This is stated now, while nothing has moved, rather than after something has.

The remaining technical risk is in the set rather than in the design. Sheets drawn over four months by two people will disagree at their edges, and finding those disagreements is what the set check is for. Time for it is in the schedule. Jason Jordan holds the standards under which the set was produced, which is what makes a check of this kind possible at all; a set drawn without that discipline cannot be checked in the time available, only redrawn.

This firm will contact Esparza-Lamb promptly should any of the above change. Questions on this report may be directed to Linda Nguyen; questions of scope, schedule, or fee should be directed to the Principal Engineer.

Project: Site Grading Design for {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}. Working session held at this office. Minutes taken by Jason Jordan and circulated for correction; corrections received within one week will be incorporated, after which these minutes stand as the record of the meeting.
Attendees

items: Linda Nguyen, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Jason Jordan, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Debra Vasquez, VP Finance, Esparza-Lamb

blocks.text: Discussion

The purpose of the session was to review the assembled plan set and the state of the submittal package. The set was reviewed sheet by sheet, in bound order, with the client present. Linda Nguyen ran the review. The set is complete as drawn and is in check.

The set check has turned up the disagreements it was expected to turn up, all of them at the match lines and none of them in the design. Six items are open. Five are lettering and callout disagreements between adjoining sheets and are corrections rather than changes. The sixth is a spot elevation that appears differently on the grading sheet and on the utility sheet. Both cannot be right. The grading sheet is correct and the utility sheet will be brought to it. Debra Vasquez asked whether this meant the utility design was wrong. It does not; the elevation was carried forward from a print that predates the confirmation of the layout, and the design against which the utilities were laid out is the correct one.

The submittal package was reviewed. The drainage computations, the earthwork estimate, and the erosion and sediment control plan are complete and are in the package. The transmittal is drafted. The package is not submitted and will not be until the set check is closed and the cover sheet is sealed. Sealing is the Principal Engineer's and is not scheduled by this meeting; the set goes to him when it is right, and it is not right until the six items are closed.

Debra Vasquez asked what remains after submittal. The answer given was the agency's review, which is outside this firm's control and for which no date can be promised, followed by this firm's response to the agency's comments through the one review cycle the agreement provides. She asked to be copied on the transmittal when the package goes. That was agreed.
No change in scope was requested at this session and none was identified. The site plan has not moved since the utility layout was confirmed, and the set as drawn reflects it.
Action Items

rows: Close the five lettering and callout items between adjoining sheets
Bring the utility sheet spot elevation to the grading sheet and recheck both
Recheck the set at the match lines after items 1 and 2 are closed
Submit the set for seal once the check is closed
Copy Debra Vasquez on the transmittal when the package is submitted
File the marked-up check prints from this session in the project folder

blocks.text: The check prints from this session are retained. They are the record of what was found and what was done about it, and they stay in the folder after the corrections are made.
